sentence: Subjective:
The patient, Mr. Roberts, is a 49-year-old male with a history of hypertension and palpitations.
He reports that his blood pressure has been high since a recent episode in the emergency room in March, which he attributes to a panic attack caused by a few cups of coffee and no breakfast.
He has a family history of high blood pressure and has been taking a new medication, Benicar HCT, which contains hydrochlorothiazide, for about a month.
He is also taking amlodipine 10 mg daily.
He checks his blood pressure at home every morning and has been seeing readings of 146-155 mmHg.
He reports that he has been following a low-sodium diet and trying to exercise regularly by walking on a treadmill.
Past medical history:
The patient has a family history of hypertension.
Review of systems:
No additional symptoms in other organs were mentioned.
Current medications:
Benicar HCT (hydrochlorothiazide) and amlodipine 10 mg daily.
Objective:
Vital signs:
Blood pressure: 146-155 mmHg
Pulse rate: [NOT MENTIONED]
Oxygen saturation: [NOT MENTIONED]
Temperature: [NOT MENTIONED]
Physical exam:
Heart and lungs sound good
No swelling in the ankles and feet
Test Results:
Potassium level: 4.0 (from 1 week ago, no recent results mentioned)
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has resistant hypertension, which is not responding to maximum doses of three medications.
Problem list: Hypertension; Resistant Hypertension; High blood pressure; Palpitations.
Plan:
I added spironolactone to his regimen to try to lower his blood pressure.
I will monitor his potassium and sodium levels in 2 weeks.
I will also check his blood pressure and perform a basic metabolic panel in 2 weeks.
If spironolactone is not effective, the next step would be to send him to the resistant hypertension clinic for further evaluation and potential treatment, including renal denervation.
Follow-up:
The patient will return in 2 weeks for follow-up and monitoring of his blood pressure and electrolyte levels.
